briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 30, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Record-Breaking Heatwaves Sweeping the Globe and Warnings of Climate Catastrophe
sections.content: The extreme heatwaves that have recently swept across Europe are becoming normalized, signaling a 'new climate era,' with cities like Paris experiencing record-breaking, worst-ever heatwaves [16, 30, 31, 161, 162]. These extreme heatwaves are identified as a cause of a sharp increase in fatalities, and with Australia also experiencing an unusually warm winter, warnings continue that climate change is rapidly approaching as a tangible disaster [3, 150, 163, 156, 157]. Climate change is impacting society in various ways, including the relationship between water and semiconductors, the formation of alternative coffee markets, difficulties for fishermen in adapting, and women's health issues [2, 14, 148, 149, 158, 159].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Adaptation and Efforts to Enhance Nuclear Power Plant Safety
sections.content: Domestically, the Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA) is actively utilizing national climate change standard scenarios and holding briefings, setting a milestone for scientific responses to the climate crisis [1, 5, 6, 20]. Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power (KHNP), in cooperation with the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), is inspecting the climate change-resilient safety designs of nuclear power plants under construction, including Shin Hanul Units 3 and 4, and strengthening international verification to enhance nuclear safety [7, 9, 11, 12, 15, 17, 18, 19, 21, 26, 27]. Furthermore, practical community-level climate adaptation efforts are actively underway, such as providing 'cool roofs' for heatwave-vulnerable populations in Busan's Seo-gu district and climate crisis adaptation education for middle school students in Yongin City [10, 24, 28, 29].

sections.title: 🌱 Acceleration of Corporate Carbon Neutrality and ESG Management, but Challenges Remain Abundant
sections.content: A number of domestic companies, primarily in the food industry, including Ottogi, Lotte Wellfood, KT, Hyundai Wia, Hyundai Rotem, and Celltrion, are successively publishing sustainability reports outlining carbon neutrality goals for 2040 or 2050, accelerating the advancement of ESG management [23, 25, 34, 35, 41-44, 47-50, 52, 54, 56, 58, 59, 62-65, 67, 70, 75, 76, 78, 80, 81, 83, 84, 86-90, 92-100]. Lotte Chilsung Beverage achieved a 17.6% reduction in carbon emissions [83], and Kumho Petrochemical anticipates a surplus from its Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage (CCUS) business [38], indicating positive developments. However, the Korea Social Responsibility Investment Forum (KoSIF) pointed out the omission of climate change from the revised Stewardship Code [13], and the cement industry is facing difficulties due to the burden of investing in carbon-neutral facilities [37], demonstrating that numerous challenges remain for achieving carbon neutrality goals [55, 57, 36].

sections.title: ⚡ Acceleration of Renewable Energy Transition and Imminent Challenges
sections.content: The government aims to secure 100GW of renewable energy through 'three mega-projects' to support the growth of AI data centers and the semiconductor industry [112, 123, 138], while the global transition is accelerating, with renewable energy projected to account for the largest share of new energy sources worldwide by 2025 [164]. Amorepacific became the first domestic private company to achieve global RE100, and Hyundai Motor also achieved 100% renewable energy in its European, North American, and Indian operations, demonstrating ongoing corporate efforts towards renewable energy transition [115, 121, 126, 129, 132, 139, 142, 145, 146]. However, concerns have been raised about the limitations of stably supplying the enormous power demand of AI data centers solely with renewable energy 24/7 [102, 103, 111, 140], and in the United States, large-scale investment projects are at risk due to delays in renewable energy permits [117, 119], clearly indicating challenges that need to be addressed.

sections.title: 🌍 Uncertainty in International Climate Policy and Governance Challenges
sections.content: Internationally, concerns have been raised that 'climate inequality' in developing countries will worsen as Europe joins former U.S. President Trump's attempts to cut off global climate response funding [4]. Furthermore, news that the World Bank plans to abandon its goal of allocating 45% of its lending resources to climate change projects adds to the uncertainty in international climate finance [147, 155]. This situation suggests that climate change is a complex challenge, representing a governance issue for pension systems and demanding new approaches to public policy [151, 154], while also emphasizing the need for expert collaboration on the security implications of climate change [153].

briefing.closing: The news shared in today's briefing highlights the global climate crisis and the various domestic and international efforts to address it. Climate change is no longer a reality we can ignore; it demands our continuous attention and concrete action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Australia’s next summer isn’t guaranteed to be the hottest yet – but it’s looking likely | Milton Speer and Lance M Leslie
articles.description: A warm start to winter is part of a global trend of extreme and unseasonable temperatures caused by global heating. Many parts of Australia have already broken early winter maximum and minimum temperature records. In southern Australia, Sydney and Melbourne had their warmest-ever starts to winter. Daily observations show both cities experienced above-average June temperatures almost every day of the month. Continue reading...

summary.phenomenon: Across Australia, a warm winter has begun, with many regions breaking early winter maximum and minimum temperature records. Particularly in southern Australia, Sydney and Melbourne experienced their warmest-ever starts to winter, recording above-average temperatures almost every day throughout the month of June.
summary.cause: This is part of a global trend of extreme and unseasonable temperatures caused by global heating.
summary.outlook: While it cannot be definitively stated that Australia's next summer will be the hottest on record, it appears highly likely.
